---
title: "Cividis Ink — Pedagogical Component Library"
subtitle: "Scratch demo (not part of the book; validates Phase 2 components in light + dark)"
author: "JoonHo Lee"
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: [cosmo, html/custom.scss]
      dark: [darkly, html/custom-dark.scss]
    css: ojs/interactive.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: false
---

This page exercises every component in the taxonomy so each can be validated in **light and dark** before any chapter uses it. It is named `_components-demo.qmd` so Quarto excludes it from the book.

## `.objectives` — chapter-opening advance organizer

::: {.objectives}
#### What you'll be able to do

- Fit a simple linear regression with `lm()` and read its coefficient table.
- Interpret the slope and intercept in substantive education-research units.
- Quantify fit with $R^2$ and the residual standard error.

::: {.ladder}
**Where this sits on the GLM ladder:** the base rung — a Normal outcome with an *identity* link. Every later model generalizes it.
:::

::: {.meta}
Prerequisites: BER 540 intro statistics · Data: the `star` study from `regdatasets`.
:::
:::

## `.worked-example` — numbered box that wraps the 3-tab panel

::: {.worked-example}
::: {.wex-header}
[Example 1.2]{.wex-tag} — Does a smaller class raise first-grade reading scores?
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output
A coefficient table goes here; read it before revealing the interpretation.

### Output Interpretation
- **Slope (13.1):** small classes score ~13 points higher on average.

### Code Explanation
```r
m1 <- lm(read ~ small, data = star)
```
:::
:::

## `.your-turn` — guided practice with collapsible solution

::: {.your-turn}
[✎ Your turn]{.yt-title} [R]{.badge-r}

Refit the model adding `aide`. **Before** running it, predict whether the `small` coefficient grows, shrinks, or stays about the same.

<details><summary>Show answer</summary>
It stays about the same (≈ 13 points): randomized assignment makes `small` and `aide` nearly uncorrelated.
</details>
:::

## `.summary` — chapter-closing recap card

::: {.summary}
### Chapter summary

::: {.summary-grid}
::: {}
##### Key takeaways
- OLS minimizes the sum of squared residuals.
- Slope = expected change in $y$ per unit of $x$.
:::
::: {}
##### New notation & terms
- $\hat\beta_1$ — fitted slope; $e_i$ — residual
:::
:::

::: {.glm}
**This model as a GLM:** Normal outcome · *identity* link · constant variance.
:::
:::

## Supporting boxes

::: {.interpretation}
A one-unit increase in prior score predicts a 0.6-point increase in the outcome, holding covariates fixed.
:::

::: {.assumption-check}
Check linearity, constant variance, and normal residuals before trusting the slope.
:::

::: {.pitfall}
Reading a slope causally from observational data ignores confounding.
:::

::: {.pronunciation}
$\beta$ is "BAY-tuh"; $\hat{y}$ is "y-hat".
:::

## `.exercises` — styled exercise container

::: {.exercises}
### Exercises

1. Using `regdatasets::star`, fit reading score on the small-class indicator and report the slope with its 95% CI. [warm-up]{.chip .easy}
   a. State the interpretation of the intercept in plain language.
   b. Re-express the slope in standard-deviation units.
2. Explain why, with randomized class assignment, the simple-regression slope equals a two-sample mean difference. [core]{.chip .med}
:::

## Definition / Theorem / Example environments

::: {#def-residual}
## Residual
The **residual** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ is the part of observation $i$'s outcome the model does not explain.
:::

::: {#thm-blue}
## Gauss–Markov
Under the standard linear-model assumptions, the OLS estimator is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE).
:::

Later chapters back-reference @def-residual and @thm-blue.
